items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: What is negative reinforcement?
items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.correct: 7 plus or minus 2 items (chunks).
items.misconception: 7 plus or minus 2 seconds.
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.correct: The visuospatial sketchpad.
items.misconception: The phonological loop.
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.correct: 65 percent.
items.misconception: 100 percent (all of them).
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.misconception: Both nature and nurture equally.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: According to Maslow, when can self-actualisation be achieved?
items.correct: Only after lower deficiency needs are largely met.
items.misconception: Anyone can self-actualise at any time through willpower.
items.why: Students miss the hierarchical structure. You cannot pursue growth needs while physiological, safety, love, and esteem needs are unmet - that's the whole point of a hierarchy.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.correct: The ego.
items.misconception: The superego (moral principle).
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: What is the independent variable?
items.correct: The variable the researcher manipulates.
items.misconception: The variable that is measured.
items.why: Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.correct: A strong negative relationship.
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: What is a random sample?
items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.misconception: Never - deception is always banned.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.q: What is vicarious reinforcement?
items.correct: Learning by observing the consequences others receive for behaviour.
items.misconception: Being directly rewarded for copying someone.
items.why: Students confuse vicarious with direct reinforcement. 'Vicarious' means experienced through someone else - you see them rewarded/punished and adjust your own behaviour accordingly.
